Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study for a couple hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I usually ended up doing most of my work in bursts when I remembered or when something was due. I'd log in, watch some videos, maybe scroll through the materials, but I wouldn't call it organized. With five online courses going on, things just kind of blurred together.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was happening. Some weeks I'd be pretty consistent, especially early in the semester when I felt motivated. But then deadlines would pile up or I'd get caught up with my extracurriculars, and suddenly I'm cramming everything into a weekend. I'm more of a reactive studier than a proactive one, if I'm being real.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification reminding me something was due or just boredom, honestly. Sometimes I'd log in because I saw classmates posting in the discussion and felt like I should check what was going on. Other times I'd get an email about new content and think, 'Okay, I should probably look at that.' It wasn't always about being productive—sometimes I just wanted to feel like I was doing something.
Q4: There was a stretch where logging in became more automatic, maybe around midterms when I was stressed about my grades. I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning with my coffee, just to see if there were updates. But that habit faded once the pressure let up. I guess I need some external push to make things stick as a routine.
Q5: The video lectures were my thing—I learn way better by listening than reading. I'd put them on and actually absorb the material pretty well. The recorded sessions where professors explained concepts step-by-step helped me understand things I'd miss in the written notes. I also liked when there were audio summaries or podcasts linked in the modules.
Q6: The reading-heavy PDFs and long text documents? I barely touched those. My eyes would glaze over after a few paragraphs. I'd download them thinking I'd get to them later, but later never really came. Discussion forums I mostly ignored too—I only posted twice because it felt performative, like I was just writing to meet a requirement rather than actually discussing anything meaningful.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the exam or if it genuinely helped me understand something confusing. If it was just busywork or something that seemed disconnected from what we were actually being tested on, I'd lose interest fast. I also valued stuff I could do while multitasking, like listening to lectures while doing other things.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt way different. Required stuff had this weight to it—like I knew it would affect my grade, so I'd at least attempt it. Optional stuff? I'd look at it and think, 'That's nice, but I've got other priorities.' Unless it seemed really interesting or someone recommended it, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download videos and listen to them on my phone while walking to my extracurricular activities or at the gym. I also used YouTube to find other explanations of concepts when the course material wasn't clicking. Sometimes I'd study with friends who weren't in my class but understood the topics, and we'd talk through stuff together. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour discussing course concepts with my roommate who's in a similar major, working through problems verbally. I'm an auditory learner, so talking things out helps me more than staring at a screen. I'd also replay downloaded lectures multiple times offline. The LMS would just show I accessed something once, but I might have engaged with it five times.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or save the slides, and then I'd barely touch the LMS version again. I preferred having things on my device so I could access them without WiFi or when I was somewhere without good internet. It was just more convenient, but I know the system wouldn't track any of that continued use.
Q12: Around week six or seven, things got rough. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars—we had a big event I was helping organize. My anxiety spiked even though I'm generally not that stressed. I started falling behind on assignments and just couldn't find the energy to catch up. It felt like I was treading water instead of actually swimming.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of zone out. I'd scroll through without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities were a big factor—I had my activities, social stuff, and five courses all demanding attention. The LMS became this thing I'd check to feel productive without actually being productive.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing in the background while I was on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Technically I 'watched' the content, but my brain wasn't really there. It's like being physically present in a lecture but mentally checked out.
Q15: Assignments were my Achilles' heel. I'd know they were important, see the deadline approaching, and still put them off. Part of it was anxiety—the longer I waited, the more overwhelming they seemed. Part of it was just that I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure. Spoiler: I don't. My assignment completion rate shows that pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline, I'd drift. With one, I'd suddenly become super active on the LMS in the 24-48 hours before. My response time averaging almost 23 hours probably reflects that—I'm submitting things right at the wire. It's not ideal, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it in time to apply it to the next thing. If I got comments on an assignment and could use them for the next one, I'd feel more motivated to engage. But sometimes feedback came too late or was too vague, and then it just felt like, 'Okay, cool, but what do I do with this?' The CBT sessions I did actually helped me process some of that frustration.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having five online courses meant I was constantly context-switching. If a course had clear weekly modules with predictable patterns, I could keep up better. When things were scattered or the expectations weren't clear, I'd disengage. I need structure handed to me because I'm not great at creating it myself.
Q19: Courses that had audio components or video-heavy content were way easier for me to engage with. I'm an auditory learner, so when everything was text-based, I struggled. Also, courses with flexible deadlines or multiple submission attempts felt less intimidating. The ones with strict, one-shot deadlines made my anxiety worse and made me more likely to avoid them.
Q20: I can totally relate to that. Sometimes you're logging in, clicking around, watching stuff, feeling like you're being a good student—but when it comes to actually sitting down and completing an assignment, there's this mental block. The LMS activity is almost like procrastination dressed up as productivity. You're busy but not accomplishing the things that actually count.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone's studying offline or they're just really efficient, their LMS activity might be low but their understanding is solid. I've had moments where I barely touched the LMS for a week but did well on an exam because I'd been studying from downloaded materials or discussing concepts with friends. The grade doesn't always match the clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of engagement. They can't tell if you're actually paying attention or just have something open in another tab. They also miss all the offline studying, the conversations, the thinking you do when you're not at a computer. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it—not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections. It's not just about showing up or clicking things; it's about being mentally present. I'd say I was engaged sometimes, but definitely not consistently.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned through listening and talking things through with people. It wouldn't capture the times I was genuinely interested in a topic and went down a rabbit hole researching it outside of class. It also wouldn't show my mental state—like how the CBT sessions helped me feel better and more capable, even if my clicking behavior didn't change much. There's a whole person behind the data points.
